critique.summary: The article discusses a game called Hostile Volume, which challenges players to set and maintain the volume at exactly 25% through various levels with intentionally frustrating user interface elements. The game combines humor and difficulty, creating a unique gaming experience.
mainPoints: Players must set the volume to exactly 25% to progress.
The game features a series of levels with intentionally bad UI and humorous challenges.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the game's mechanics and objectives appear to be accurate based on the content provided. The description of the game as creatively frustrating aligns with the typical design of games that use humor and challenge to engage players.
considerations: The article does not mention the potential accessibility issues for players who may struggle with the game's UI design.
No information is provided about the game's reception or reviews from players.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the frustrating and often humorous aspects of poorly designed volume controls in games and applications. Several commenters share their experiences with specific levels of the game and offer insights into the design flaws that mimic real-world UI issues.

comments.author: dsmason321
comments.text: Level 17 is NOT bugged. The slider is backward and the volume nonresponsive. Its a planned feature.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a clear and accurate explanation of a specific game mechanic, helping to clarify a common misconception.

comments.author: graypegg
comments.text: This is not an issue at all, but when ever I come across something like it, I like to poke at the frontend in dev tools a bit.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a thoughtful approach to exploring UI issues, though it may downplay the frustrations others experience.

comments.author: pimlottc
comments.text:
This works for almost all levels:

   for (i = 0; i < 50; i++) { document.querySelector("#l3-down").click(); }
comments.analysis: While the comment offers a clever workaround, it may not address the broader design issues that players are discussing.

comments.author: TheLNL
comments.text: Finished the game. It was fun to play. I got stuck for a while on the opposite level where the display doesn't update...
comments.analysis: This comment shares a personal experience and adds to the discussion about gameplay, though it lacks critical analysis of the UI design.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text: There are two types of volume slider I've encountered thus far, "too logarithmic", and "not logarithmic enough".
comments.analysis: The comment presents an interesting perspective on volume sliders but does not engage deeply with the specific game context.

comments.author: jonathanlydall
comments.text: My favourite bad volume control was in Real Player around 1997 where changing the volume in the application actually changed...
comments.analysis: While nostalgic and relevant, this comment does not directly contribute to the current discussion about the game.

comments.author: burgerone
comments.text: Prwtty neat. Unfortunately wasn't able to solve the UI desync one :/
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a personal experience but lacks depth in analysis or constructive feedback.

comments.author: jimkleiber
comments.text:
Got an error on Level 17, just a heads up.

Love the game, btw.

comments.text: Laughed out loud but gave up at level 5

comments.analysis: This comment is likely inaccurate and does not contribute meaningfully to the discussion.

comments.text: These mostly seem to be variations of "takes a long time / is tedious" rather than "annoying/fiddly...

comments.author: Pipe94
comments.text: somehow i'm amazed and annoyed at the same time

comments.author: DrSiemer
comments.text: Plenty of annoyance in here for sure. Looks like 17 cannot be finished on mobile though...
comments.analysis: While it notes a specific issue, it does not provide a deeper analysis of the design flaws.

comments.author: Findecanor
comments.text:
I have encountered the rate-limited spinner (#8) and the self-resizing slider (#5) in real desktop UIs.

#3 are almost like...

comments.text: The worst volume control UI in the world (2017): https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=27819384

comments.text: Meanwhile, iPhone is still using this design https://xkcd.com/1884/
